text: The video shows the patient's abdomen filled with a large myoma, with visible signs of ovarian cysts.
The surgeon shows the patient's abdomen at the end of the surgery, which appears flat and without any distension, indicating successful completion of the procedure.
The surgeon inserts a Veress needle at Palmer's point on the abdomen, marking the initial step of the laparoscopic procedure.

The surgeon performs an irrigation test, suction test, and hanging drop test on the abdomen after addressing a large fibroid. The varus needle is introduced through the Palmer's point, located two centimeters above the costal margin, known as Mishra's point. The abdomen is insufflated to a pressure of eighteen millimeters of mercury to create more space for the procedure. Isoflurane anesthesia is used to avoid myocardial toxicity, and a DVT pump is applied to the patient.

The surgeon enlarges the incision to accommodate an 11 mm optical trocar, following the completion of pneumoperitoneum. This step is crucial to prevent compression of the vena cava by the large myoma, which could reduce venous return and increase the risk of deep vein thrombosis.

The surgeon introduces the first trocar at Palmer's point, with the abdomen fully insufflated. The telescope is inserted and navigated laterally through the paracolic gutter to avoid the fibroid. The second port is placed along the anterior axillary line, utilizing the baseball diamond concept for optimal access.

The surgeon introduces a second port approximately ten centimeters away from the initial port under direct vision. A third port is also placed on the same line in the epigastric region, about three centimeters below and to the left of the xiphoid process. This setup is similar to the baseball diamond configuration used in cholecystectomy, allowing for the transfer of the telescope from Palmer's point to the epigastric region.

All ports are positioned, and the surgeon holds a grasper in each hand. The patient's head is tilted down and to the right to create space for the telescope. A large myoma is visible, occupying most of the abdominal cavity up to the costal margin.

The laparoscopic telescope is positioned at the GP sternum, providing a view of the fibroid extending above the costal margin. The fibroid is visibly pedunculated with a pedicle, and the surgeon navigates down the paracolic gutter to assess the fibroid's torsion, which caused severe pain for the patient.
The surgeon manipulates the torted fibroid, which is pressing against the cecum and small intestine. The round ligament on the right side is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon attempts to detort the ovary and fallopian tube, which are visibly twisted. Despite multiple attempts at detorsion using a grasper, complete detorsion is challenging due to the presence of a large myoma. The fallopian tube on the opposite side is also visible.

The surgeon manipulates the large myoma, which is visibly moving within the abdominal cavity. An extracorporeal Misrash knot is being applied to the pedicle at the base of the myoma. The telescope has been repositioned to provide a view from the lower part of the abdomen.

The surgeon changes the telescope position to visualize the pedicle extending from below. A number one Y-Curl suture is encircled around the pedicle and brought out from the opposite side. The surgeon manipulates the suture from left to right, with the iliopsoas and round ligaments visible, pushing towards the upper abdomen. The suture is then tied using an extracorporeal Misra's knot, capable of securing pedicles up to 22 millimeters.

The assistant uses a mama screw to elevate the myoma, defining the pedicle more clearly. The surgeon removes the suture and ties an extracorporeal Misra knot outside the body, ensuring the knot configuration is tightened as one-one-one-one-one.

The surgeon tightens an extracorporeal knot over the pedicle, forming a visible dumbbell shape. The knot is secured with multiple winds and locks, providing relief by stabilizing the pedunculated structure amidst surrounding anatomical challenges.

The surgeon begins enucleation of the intramural myoma using a ligature and harmonic device, starting approximately one centimeter above the previously tied knot. The instruments are used to detach the myoma from surrounding tissue.

The telescope is positioned in the lumbar area to improve visibility, as the previous palmar point approach was obstructed by the myoma. The surgeon uses a ligature to coagulate and separate the tissue, carefully managing potential bleeding.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect tissue, demonstrating the separation of anatomical structures with the new harmonic probe.

The surgeon encounters minor bleeding and oozing while dissecting a vascular myoma with large blood vessels. A combination of ligature, used as a vessel sealer, and harmonic scalpel, used as a dissector, is employed to carefully separate the myoma.

The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to carefully separate and cut the pedicle of the myoma, lifting it over the vibrating jaw of the harmonic to ensure no vessels are left untouched, minimizing the risk of bleeding.

During the laparoscopic myomectomy, the surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to slowly separate the myoma from surrounding tissue. The myoma is gradually being detached, requiring patience due to the slow morselation process, which is limited to forty grams per minute.

The surgeon uses an extracorporeal knot to control bleeding while cutting through a large myoma. Big vessels are visible and are being coagulated to prevent bleeding, with energy devices and the knot working together to maintain hemostasis.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw to reposition and pull the large myoma over the anterior surface of the liver, accessing it through the epigastric port to facilitate continued myomectomy.

Two myoma screws are used to pull the large myoma over the anterior surface of the liver, one inserted through the epigastric port and the other through the Palmer's port. The surgeon prepares to cut the entire pedicle, which includes part of the uterus, while an extracorporeal knot is applied to control bleeding.
The surgeon continues to cut the pedicle of the myoma, with approximately seventy-five percent of the pedicle already severed. The remaining pedicle is being carefully dissected laparoscopically.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers above the right ovary, ensuring its protection while continuing to cut the remaining pedicle of the large myoma. The ovary is visible and remains intact as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to perform desiccation on the cut edge of the myoma, addressing oozing to achieve hemostasis before detaching the myoma completely. This step is crucial to prevent increased bleeding once the tension on the pedicle is released.

The surgeon carefully detaches the last part of the myoma, ensuring hemostasis is achieved to prevent oozing. The decision regarding the preservation or removal of the ovary and potential salpingectomy is considered based on the patient's age.

An instrument is used to elevate the pedicle, preventing accidental contact with underlying bowel. A knot is visibly tightened, forming a dumbbell shape, as the surgeon prepares to cut the last part of the pedicle.

The surgeon ties a pre-tied Mistra's knot around the large pedicle of the myoma, reinforcing the initial knot to prevent re-bleeding. The knot is tightened securely at the base of the pedicle, ensuring hemostasis.
The surgeon tightens the knot securely, augmenting the first knot on the pedicle. After cutting the knot, the act of morselation begins, with the tissue being pulled towards the gallbladder.

The surgeon identifies a para-ovarian cyst on the left side of the ovary and proceeds to remove it. The infundibulo-ovarian ligament is cut, and the fallopian tube on the same side is also removed, as the patient is not planning future pregnancies.

The surgeon removes a para ovarian cyst by cutting the infundibulopelvic ligament. The major salpings are exposed, and six millimeters of the fallopian tube are left near the uterus before the tube is removed.
The surgeon completes the ovarian cystectomy and proceeds to use a morcellator to fragment and remove the tube and cyst from the abdominal cavity, ensuring no small tissue remnants are left inside.

The surgeon begins morselating the myoma, which is positioned in the epigastric region. The myoma screw is located in the lumbar area to facilitate better morselation. Due to the large size of the myoma, the entire structure is not visible at once, requiring continuous adjustment of the telescope to maintain a panoramic view during the morselation process.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic morselation of the myoma, with visible fragments being gradually reduced and extracted over time. The process is ongoing, highlighting the extensive duration required for complete morselation.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the tissue towards the morselator during the laparoscopic myomectomy, ensuring the morselator remains stationary. The video transitions to the cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy with the telescope positioned in the lumbar area and instruments in the Palmer's point and epigastric region, deviating from the typical port placement. The gallbladder is visible, and the procedure progresses despite challenging ergonomics due to unconventional instrument angles.

The surgeon uses a port with the telescope positioned in the lumbar area to create a posterior peritoneal window above the Rouviere's sulcus. Anteromedial traction is applied to separate the posterior peritoneum, resembling the mouth of an elephant from the left side. The dissection reveals the critical view of safety, with two structures clearly visible leading to the gallbladder and the inferior surface of the liver exposed.
The surgeon identifies the artery and vein, creating a window between them. The left lobe of the liver is visible, obstructing the view due to the telescope's positioning.

The surgeon performs peritoneal dissection, stripping the anterior peritoneum to create a window between the artery and duct. The interior window is visible, along with the window between the duct and the inferior surface of the liver.
The surgeon performs mechanical stripping to enlarge the window between the common bile duct (CBD) and the inferior surface of the liver, which is visible above the Rouvière's sulcus.

The clip applicator is positioned at Palmer's point to secure the gallbladder. The posterior window is enlarged at the porta hepatis near the ruviate sulcus, ensuring the inferior surface of the liver is clearly visible. The surgeon performs posterior peritoneal dissection to further separate the gallbladder from the liver.
The surgeon enlarges the anterior and posterior windows, further skeletonizing the structures around the gallbladder, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon enlarges the anterior window from the opposite side, ensuring clear visibility of both the cystic artery and duct, with no tenting of the common bile duct (CBD).
The surgeon positions the titanium clip applicator to secure the cystic duct, which is located below the cystic artery. Both structures are clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon applies a titanium clip over the cystic duct, avoiding lateral clipping due to its average size and slight dilation. The common hepatic duct is visible in the foreground. A single clip is applied to the artery, which will be coagulated using a harmonic scalpel before cutting.
The surgeon uses a Soundreach probe to cut the cystic duct, following the application of titanium clips on the duct and artery.

The surgeon carefully separates the posterior peritoneum while applying anteromedial traction to the gallbladder. The right shoulder of the gallbladder is cut using anterolateral traction to prevent rupture and facilitate separation from the liver. The surgical field is visually challenging due to modified instrument and telescope directions.

The gallbladder is being carefully separated from the liver bed, with visible progress as the gallbladder is gradually lifted away. The surgical field shows the gallbladder moving smoothly, indicating successful dissection.
The surgeon completes the removal of the gallbladder during the cholecystectomy procedure. Some blood is visible in the upper abdomen, attributed to the myomectomy, and is being suctioned out.
The surgeon inspects the myoma pedicle, confirming its integrity and absence of any issues.

The surgeon lifts the appendix and begins the laparoscopic appendectomy using a ligature harmonic device. The gallbladder is also held in place to prevent it from being missed during the procedure. The ligature harmonic is used to coagulate and cut the mesoappendix, pulling it towards the cecum.

The surgeon mobilizes the ileum from the paracolic gutter, performing lateral peritoneum mobilization. The major appendix is cut at its base, reaching the tinea coli to ensure complete removal of the appendix.

The surgeon uses a pre-tied Misra knot, placing it through a loop and tying it at the base of the cecum. The knot pusher is positioned over the cecum, causing the knot to shrink and secure the base. The harmonic device is used to cut and seal the tissue, burning the mucosa. Claw forceps are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a morcellator port to remove the appendix and gallbladder, both extracted through the same 15mm port. Following the removal, the surgical field is inspected, particularly the cul-de-sac, where collected blood is suctioned out. The bowel is lifted to facilitate inspection, ensuring no fresh bleeding is present, only residual blood from the myomectomy.

The surgeon performs final suction of collected blood in the cul-de-sac, inspecting the sacral promontory and visible right ureter. The rectum is lifted to check for fragmented myoma tissue in the pararectal area. The procedure concludes with the closure of the fifteen mm port, while the Palmer's port remains open as it is above the costal margin.

The surgeon uses a cobbler's needle to close the 15mm port, ensuring all layers except the skin are included in the closure to prevent incisional hernia. The abdomen appears lax after the removal of a large fibroid, resembling a full-term pregnancy at the start of the procedure.
